reasoning: The EU imported roughly 43-45 bcm of Russian gas in 2023, an average of ~11 bcm per quarter, comprising LNG (~4.5 bcm/q) and pipeline imports via TurkStream and Ukraine (~7.5 bcm/q). In January 2025, the Russia-Ukraine transit agreement expired, cutting off approximately 15 bcm/year (3.8 bcm/q) of pipeline capacity. Consequently, remaining physical routes for Russian gas to the EU are restricted to the European string of TurkStream (max ~15.75 bcm/yr, or ~4 bcm/q) and Western LNG exports (mainly Yamal LNG and Portovaya, physically maxing out around 24 bcm/yr, or ~6 bcm/q). Even if these remaining sources run at 100% capacity and send every drop exclusively to the EU, they sum to roughly 10 bcm/q. Reaching 15 bcm in any quarter would require reopening dormant/sanctioned pipelines like Yamal-Europe, which is highly implausible given current geopolitics and EU phase-out policies. The physical constraints make hitting exactly/above 15,000 million cubic meters virtually impossible for 2025.
